Count occurrences of each element. Returns a map element -> count. frequencies([1, 1, 2, 3, 3, 3]) // => {1 2, 2 1, 3 3}

group-by(f, coll)
beagle.iter.bg:303

Group elements by f's return value. Returns a map key -> vector of elements. group-by(fn(x) { x % 2 }, [1, 2, 3, 4]) // => {1 [1, 3], 0 [2, 4]}

count-by(f, coll)
beagle.iter.bg:323

Count elements by f's return value. Returns a map key -> count. count-by(fn(x) { x % 2 }, [1, 2, 3, 4, 5]) // => {1 3, 0 2}

repeat(n, x)
beagle.iter.bg:370

A vector containing x repeated n times. repeat(3, "a") // => ["a", "a", "a"]

cycle-take(n, coll)
beagle.iter.bg:382

Take n elements by cycling through coll repeatedly. cycle-take(7, [1, 2, 3]) // => [1, 2, 3, 1, 2, 3, 1]

enumerate(coll)
beagle.iter.bg:404

Pair each element with its 0-based index. enumerate(["a", "b", "c"]) // => [[0, "a"], [1, "b"], [2, "c"]]

sum(coll)
beagle.iter.bg:422

Sum of all elements (0 for empty). sum([1, 2, 3, 4]) // => 10

product(coll)
beagle.iter.bg:434

Product of all elements (1 for empty). product([1, 2, 3, 4]) // => 24

min-by(f, coll)
beagle.iter.bg:446

Element with the smallest f value (first such on ties); null if empty. min-by(fn(s) { length(s) }, ["abc", "a", "ab"]) // => "a"

max-by(f, coll)
beagle.iter.bg:469

Element with the largest f value (first such on ties); null if empty. max-by(fn(s) { length(s) }, ["a", "abc", "ab"]) // => "abc"

accumulate(f, init, coll)
beagle.iter.bg:498

Running fold prefixes of f over coll, starting from init. Alias of reductions (the itertools.accumulate / reductions concept). The output includes the initial value as its first element, then each successive accumulation. accumulate(fn(a, x) { a + x }, 0, [1, 2, 3]) // => [0, 1, 3, 6]

cartesian-product(vec-of-vecs)
beagle.iter.bg:514

Cartesian product of a vector of vectors. Returns a vector of tuples (each tuple itself a vector), with the rightmost input varying fastest, matching python's itertools.product.

NOTE ON NAMING: this is named cartesian-product rather than product because beagle.iter/product already exists as the numeric product of a collection (product([1,2,3,4]) => 24). Keeping both names preserves the existing public API.

cartesian-product([[1, 2], [3, 4]]) // => [[1, 3], [1, 4], [2, 3], [2, 4]] cartesian-product([]) // => [[]] (empty product is one empty tuple) cartesian-product([[1, 2], []]) // => [] (any empty factor => no tuples)

combinations(coll, k)
beagle.iter.bg:547

All k-element combinations of vec, in lexicographic index order, no repeats, preserving the input order within each combination. Matches itertools.combinations. combinations([1, 2, 3, 4], 2) // => [[1, 2], [1, 3], [1, 4], [2, 3], [2, 4], [3, 4]] combinations(x, 0) // => [[]] combinations(x, k) where k > length(x) // => []

combinations-from(v, n, k, start)
beagle.iter.bg:560

Recursive helper for combinations: build all k-element combinations drawn from indices start..n of v, in lexicographic index order, as element vectors.

push-front-elem(x, vec)
beagle.iter.bg:584

Prepend element x to vector vec, returning a new vector with x at index 0.

combinations-with-replacement(coll, k)
beagle.iter.bg:600

All k-element combinations of coll allowing repeated elements, in lexicographic index order, preserving input order within each combination. Matches itertools.combinations_with_replacement. combinations-with-replacement([1, 2, 3], 2) // => [[1, 1], [1, 2], [1, 3], [2, 2], [2, 3], [3, 3]] combinations-with-replacement(x, 0) // => [[]] combinations-with-replacement([], k) where k > 0 // => []

combinations-wr-from(v, n, k, start)
beagle.iter.bg:614

Recursive helper for combinations-with-replacement: build all k-element combinations drawn from indices start..n of v, allowing each index to repeat, in lexicographic index order, as element vectors.

permutations-of(v, k)
beagle.iter.bg:656

Helper for permutations: all k-length permutations of vector v in itertools order. Throws if k < 0; returns [] when k > length(v).

permutations-rec(v, n, k, used)
beagle.iter.bg:679

Recursive helper for permutations-of: place each still-unused index in turn (tracked by the boolean used vector) to build k-length element tuples.

2 undocumented
  • range(start, end)range(start, end, step)arities 2, 3
  • permutations(coll)permutations(coll, k)arities 1, 2

#beagle.json

37/38 documented
cur-code(c)
beagle.json.bg:49

Char code at the cursor's current position, or -1 at end of input.

at-end?(c)
beagle.json.bg:58

True when the cursor has consumed the entire source string.

ws-code?(code)
beagle.json.bg:63

True when code is a JSON whitespace byte (space, tab, LF, or CR).

skip-ws(c)
beagle.json.bg:68

Advance the cursor past any run of JSON whitespace.

parse-error(c, msg)
beagle.json.bg:75

Throw a beagle.json/parse error tagged with msg and the cursor position.

parse-document(c)
beagle.json.bg:82

Parse a complete JSON document (one value plus surrounding whitespace). Throws if any non-whitespace remains after the value.

parse-value(c)
beagle.json.bg:95

Parse a single JSON value at the cursor, dispatching on the leading byte (object, array, string, true/false/null literal, or number).

parse-literal(c, word, value)
beagle.json.bg:136

Match the exact keyword word (true/false/null) at the cursor and return value; throws if the text does not match.

parse-object(c)
beagle.json.bg:154

Parse a JSON object (cursor at the opening {) into a map. Keys become keywords or stay strings depending on the cursor's keyword-keys flag.

parse-array(c)
beagle.json.bg:201

Parse a JSON array (cursor at the opening [) into a vector.

parse-string(c)
beagle.json.bg:233

Parse a JSON string (cursor at the opening quote), decoding escapes, into a String. Throws on an unterminated string or bad escape.

parse-escape(c, out)
beagle.json.bg:265

Decode the backslash escape at the cursor (\", \\, \/, \n, \t, \r, \b, \f, or \uXXXX) and append its byte(s) to out.

parse-unicode-escape(c, out)
beagle.json.bg:317

Parse a \uXXXX escape (cursor at the u), combining a high/low surrogate pair when present, and append the code point as UTF-8 to out.

read-hex4(c)
beagle.json.bg:342

Read exactly 4 hex digits at the cursor and return their integer value; throws on a short or non-hex sequence.

hex-digit-value(code)
beagle.json.bg:363

Value (0-15) of a hex-digit char code (0-9, a-f, A-F), or -1 if not hex.

encode-utf8(out, cp)
beagle.json.bg:381

Encode Unicode code point cp as 1-4 UTF-8 bytes appended to out.

digit-code?(code)
beagle.json.bg:404

True when code is an ASCII digit '0'-'9'.

parse-number(c)
beagle.json.bg:412

Scan a JSON number at the cursor, returning an Int when it has no fraction or exponent and a Float otherwise. Throws on malformed numbers.

parse-int-text(c, text)
beagle.json.bg:478

Parse an integer numeric substring into a tagged Int, degrading to a Float approximation when the value overflows the 2^60-1 tagged-Int range.

to-float-checked(c, text)
beagle.json.bg:516

Parse an already-scanner-validated float substring into a Float (thin wrapper over parse-float-text).

parse-float-text(s)
beagle.json.bg:529

Parse a validated numeric substring s into a Float via the native correctly-rounded parser. Throws if the native parser unexpectedly rejects it.

parse(s)
beagle.json.bg:547

Parse a JSON string into a Beagle value. Object keys become keywords.

Examples: parse("{\"a\":[1,2,3],\"b\":true,\"c\":null}") // => {:a [1, 2, 3], :b true, :c null}

parse-with-string-keys(s)
beagle.json.bg:556

Parse a JSON string into a Beagle value, keeping object keys as Strings.

Examples: parse-with-string-keys("{\"a\":1}") // => {"a" 1}

parse-result(s)
beagle.json.bg:568

Parse a JSON string without throwing: returns Result.Ok { value } on success, or Result.Err { error } carrying the parse error message instead of throwing. Object keys become keywords, like parse.

Examples: parse-result("{\"a\":1}") // => Result.Ok { value: {:a 1} } parse-result("{") // => Result.Err { error: "beagle.json/parse: ..." }

stringify(value)
beagle.json.bg:584

Serialize a Beagle value to compact JSON.

Examples: stringify({:a 1, :b [1, 2]}) // => {"a":1,"b":[1,2]}

stringify-sorted(value)
beagle.json.bg:618

Serialize a Beagle value to compact JSON with object keys emitted in sorted (canonical) order at every nesting level. Useful for stable, comparable output such as hashing or diffing two equivalent values.

Examples: stringify-sorted({:b 1, :a 2}) // => {"a":2,"b":1}

write-value-sorted(out, value, indent, depth)
beagle.json.bg:628

Write value as JSON into out like write-value, but recursing through the sorted writers so object keys are emitted in sorted order at every depth.

write-array-sorted(out, vec, indent, depth)
beagle.json.bg:675

Write vector vec as a JSON array into out like write-array, but recursing through write-value-sorted so nested object keys stay sorted.

write-object-sorted(out, m, indent, depth)
beagle.json.bg:698

Write map m as a JSON object into out like write-object, but with keys emitted in sorted order (by their JSON string form) and values recursing through write-value-sorted.

write-value(out, value, indent, depth)
beagle.json.bg:728

Write value as JSON into builder out. indent < 0 means compact; depth is the current nesting level. Throws on unserializable value types.

write-newline-indent(out, indent, depth)
beagle.json.bg:778

In pretty mode (indent >= 0) write a newline plus indent * depth spaces; a no-op in compact mode.

write-array(out, vec, indent, depth)
beagle.json.bg:790

Write vector vec as a JSON array into out, honoring indent/depth.

write-object(out, m, indent, depth)
beagle.json.bg:812

Write map m as a JSON object into out, honoring indent/depth; keys are stringified via key->string.

key->string(k)
beagle.json.bg:841

Convert a map key (keyword, string, or other) to its JSON string form.

write-string(out, s)
beagle.json.bg:856

Write string s into out as a JSON string literal, including the surrounding quotes and escaping of special and control characters.

write-control-escape(out, code)
beagle.json.bg:908

Write control char code as a \u00XX escape sequence into out.

hex-char(d)
beagle.json.bg:918

ASCII char code of the lowercase hex digit for d (0-15).

1 undocumented
  • stringify-pretty(value)stringify-pretty(value, indent)arities 1, 2

#beagle.regex

9/9 documented
compile(pattern)
builtin

Compile a regular expression pattern.

Returns a Regex object that can be used with other regex functions.

Examples: (let re (regex/compile "[0-9]+"))

matches?(regex, string)
builtin

Check if the entire string matches the regex.

Examples: (regex/matches? (regex/compile "[0-9]+") "123") ; => true (regex/matches? (regex/compile "[0-9]+") "abc") ; => false

find(regex, string)
builtin

Find the first match in the string.

Returns a map with :start, :end, and :match keys, or null if no match.

Examples: (regex/find (regex/compile "[0-9]+") "abc123def") ; => {:start 3 :end 6 :match "123"}

find-all(regex, string)
builtin

Find all matches in the string.

Returns a vector of match maps.

Examples: (regex/find-all (regex/compile "[0-9]+") "a1b2c3") ; => [{:start 1 :end 2 :match "1"} ...]

replace(regex, string, replacement)
builtin

Replace the first match in the string with the replacement.

Examples: (regex/replace (regex/compile "[0-9]+") "a1b2c3" "X") ; => "aXb2c3"

replace-all(regex, string, replacement)
builtin

Replace all matches in the string with the replacement.

Examples: (regex/replace-all (regex/compile "[0-9]+") "a1b2c3" "X") ; => "aXbXcX"

split(regex, string)
builtin

Split a string by the regex pattern.

Returns a vector of strings.

Examples: (regex/split (regex/compile ",\\s*") "a, b, c") ; => ["a" "b" "c"]

captures(regex, string)
builtin

Get capture groups from the first match.

Returns a vector of captured strings (index 0 is the full match), or null if no match.

Examples: (regex/captures (regex/compile "(\\w+)@(\\w+)") "user@host") ; => ["user@host" "user" "host"]

is-regex?(value)
builtin

Check if a value is a compiled regex.

Examples: (regex/is-regex? (regex/compile "test")) ; => true (regex/is-regex? "test") ; => false

#beagle.reflect

22/22 documented
type-of(value)
builtin

Get a type descriptor for any value.

Returns a type descriptor that can be used with other reflect functions.

Examples: (reflect/type-of 42) ; => <type Int> (reflect/type-of "hello") ; => <type String>

kind(descriptor)
builtin

Get the type kind from a type descriptor.

Returns a keyword: :struct, :enum, :function, or :primitive.

Examples: (reflect/kind (reflect/type-of some-struct)) ; => :struct

name(descriptor)
builtin

Get the type name from a type descriptor.

Returns the name as a string.

Examples: (reflect/name (reflect/type-of 42)) ; => "Int"

doc(descriptor)
builtin

Get the docstring for a type or function.

Returns the documentation string or null if none available.

Examples: (reflect/doc (reflect/type-of some-fn)) ; => "Documentation..." (reflect/doc (reflect/type-of 42)) ; => null

fields(descriptor)
builtin

Get the field names for a struct type.

Returns a vector of field names, or null for non-struct types.

Examples: (reflect/fields (reflect/type-of my-struct)) ; => ["field1" "field2"]

variants(descriptor)
builtin

Get the variant names for an enum type.

Returns a vector of variant names, or null for non-enum types.

Examples: (reflect/variants (reflect/type-of Result.Ok)) ; => ["Ok" "Err"]

args(descriptor)
builtin

Get the argument names for a function.

Returns a vector of argument names, or null for non-function types.

Examples: (reflect/args (reflect/type-of println)) ; => ["value"]

variadic?(descriptor)
builtin

Check if a function accepts variable arguments.

Returns true if the function is variadic, false otherwise.

Examples: (reflect/variadic? (reflect/type-of +)) ; => true

info(descriptor)
builtin

Get complete type information as a map.

Returns a map containing all available metadata about the type, including kind, name, docstring, fields/variants/args as appropriate.

Examples: (reflect/info (reflect/type-of my-fn)) ; => {:kind :function :name "my-fn" :args [...] ...}

struct?(value)
builtin

Check if a value is a struct type or instance.

Examples: (reflect/struct? my-struct-instance) ; => true (reflect/struct? 42) ; => false

enum?(value)
builtin

Check if a value is an enum type or variant.

Examples: (reflect/enum? Result.Ok) ; => true (reflect/enum? 42) ; => false

function?(value)
builtin

Check if a value is a function.

Examples: (reflect/function? println) ; => true (reflect/function? 42) ; => false

primitive?(value)
builtin

Check if a value is a primitive type (Int, Float, String, Bool, Null).

Examples: (reflect/primitive? 42) ; => true (reflect/primitive? [1 2 3]) ; => false

namespace-members(namespace-name)
builtin

List all members defined in a namespace.

Returns a vector of member names.

Examples: (reflect/namespace-members "beagle.core") ; => ["println" "map" ...]

all-namespaces()
builtin

List all namespace names in the runtime.

Returns a vector of namespace name strings.

Examples: (reflect/all-namespaces) ; => ["beagle.core" "beagle.fs" ...]

apropos(query)
builtin

Search for functions by name or docstring substring.

Returns a vector of matching function names.

Examples: (reflect/apropos "print") ; => ["println" "print" ...]

namespace-info(namespace-name)
builtin

Get detailed information about a namespace.

Returns a map with the namespace's functions, structs, and enums.

Examples: (reflect/namespace-info "beagle.core") ; => {:functions [...] :structs [...] :enums [...]}

source(value)
builtin

Return the original source text for a definition.

Accepts a function value, struct/enum value, or type descriptor. Returns null if no source text is stored (builtins, foreign functions, or anonymous closures).

This is the round-trippable counterpart to eval: read source, edit it, and re-eval the new text to redefine the function in place.

Examples: (reflect/source greet) ; => "fn greet(name) {\n println(\"Hello, \" + name)\n}"

namespace-source(namespace-name)
builtin

Return the concatenated source text of every definition in a namespace.

Members without stored source (builtins, foreign fns) are skipped. Definitions are emitted in registration order: structs first, then enums, then functions. Returns null if the namespace has no members with source.

Examples: (reflect/namespace-source "my.module") ; => "struct Point { x, y }\n\nfn distance(a, b) { ... }"

location(value)
builtin

Return the on-disk location of a definition as a map {:file, :byte-start, :byte-end, :line-start, :line-end}, or null.

Accepts the same kinds of values as reflect/source: function, struct/enum value or type descriptor. The byte range covers the full block as it lives on disk, including any preceding /// doc comment lines. REPL/eval definitions, builtins, and foreign functions return null.

Pair with reflect/write-source to persist edits back to disk.

Examples: (reflect/location greet) ; => {:file "my.bg" :byte-start 120 :byte-end 200 :line-start 5 :line-end 8}

write-source(value, new-text)
builtin

Persist an edited definition back to its source file and re-register it in the runtime.

Reads the file at the definition's on-disk location, verifies the bytes still match what was loaded (aborting if the file drifted), splices new-text in at the recorded byte range, writes the file, and re-compiles the new text in the definition's namespace so subsequent reflect/source returns it. Byte ranges of other definitions in the same file are shifted to account for the length change.

Throws a runtime error (kind write-source) when the value has no on-disk origin, the file has drifted, I/O fails, or the new text doesn't parse. Returns true on success.

Examples: (reflect/write-source greet "fn greet(name) { println(\"hi \" ++ name) }")

persist(namespace, text)
builtin

Persist one or more top-level definitions to disk, dispatching each as an update or an append.

Parses text to discover top-level fn/struct/enum definitions. For each def, if namespace/<name> already has an on-disk location, it is spliced in place (drift-checked against the stored source text first); otherwise the fragment is appended to the namespace's source file. All drift checks run up-front, so either every update succeeds or nothing is written.

Returns a vector of maps [{:name, :action}] where :action is "updated" or "appended". Throws on parse failure, drift, I/O error, or compile error.

Examples: (reflect/persist "my.module" "fn greet(name) { println(\"hi \" ++ name) }") ; => [{:name "my.module/greet" :action "appended"}]

#beagle.timer

8/10 documented
sleep(ms)
beagle.timer.bg:45

Sleep for a number of milliseconds.

Must be called within a Timer effect handler block.

Examples: timer/sleep(100) // => null

now()
beagle.timer.bg:55

Get current time in nanoseconds since epoch.

Examples: let start = timer/now() // ... do work ... let elapsed = timer/now() - start

timeout(ms, future)
beagle.timer.bg:94

Wrap a future with a timeout.

Returns Result.Ok if completed before timeout, or Result.Err with Timeout error. Requires both Timer and Async handlers to be installed.

Examples: match timer/timeout(1000, my-future) { Result.Ok { value } => value, Result.Err { error } => "timed out" }

deadline(ms)
beagle.timer.bg:137

Create a deadline (absolute time in nanoseconds from now).

Useful for setting a single deadline across multiple operations.

Examples: let d = timer/deadline(5000) // => 5000000000

deadline-passed?(deadline-time)
beagle.timer.bg:142

Check if a deadline has passed.

deadline-remaining(deadline-time)
beagle.timer.bg:147

Get remaining milliseconds until a deadline (0 if passed).

blocking-sleep(ms)
beagle.timer.bg:173

Sleep for a number of milliseconds synchronously.

No handler required. Blocks the current thread.

Examples: timer/blocking-sleep(100) // => null

blocking-now()
beagle.timer.bg:180

Get current time in nanoseconds synchronously.

No handler required.

2 undocumented
  • run-blocking(thunk)
  • BlockingTimerHandler_handle(self, op, resume)method

#beagle.ansi

54/54 documented
enabled?()
beagle.ansi.bg:39

True if ANSI escapes are currently emitted (false when NO_COLOR disabled them or set-enabled!(false) was called).

set-enabled!(on)
beagle.ansi.bg:45

Enable or disable ANSI escape emission at runtime. When disabled, every styling function returns its text unchanged.

wrap(code, s)
beagle.ansi.bg:55

Wrap s in a single SGR escape: ESC[<code>m s ESC[0m. Returns a new string.

style(s, codes)
beagle.ansi.bg:68

Apply a vector of SGR codes as one combined escape; an empty codes returns s unchanged.

bold(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:100

Render s bold (SGR 1).

dim(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:102

Render s dim/faint (SGR 2).

italic(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:104

Render s italic (SGR 3).

underline(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:106

Underline s (SGR 4).

blink(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:108

Render s blinking (SGR 5).

reverse(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:110

Swap foreground/background for s (reverse video, SGR 7).

hidden(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:112

Render s hidden/invisible (SGR 8).

strikethrough(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:114

Render s with a strikethrough line (SGR 9).

black(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:121

Black foreground (SGR 30).

red(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:123

Red foreground (SGR 31).

green(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:125

Green foreground (SGR 32).

yellow(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:127

Yellow foreground (SGR 33).

blue(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:129

Blue foreground (SGR 34).

magenta(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:131

Magenta foreground (SGR 35).

cyan(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:133

Cyan foreground (SGR 36).

white(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:135

White foreground (SGR 37).

bright-black(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:142

Bright black foreground (SGR 90).

bright-red(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:144

Bright red foreground (SGR 91).

bright-green(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:146

Bright green foreground (SGR 92).

bright-yellow(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:148

Bright yellow foreground (SGR 93).

bright-blue(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:150

Bright blue foreground (SGR 94).

bright-magenta(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:152

Bright magenta foreground (SGR 95).

bright-cyan(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:154

Bright cyan foreground (SGR 96).

bright-white(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:156

Bright white foreground (SGR 97).

gray(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:160

Alias for bright-black (SGR 90).

grey(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:162

Alias for bright-black (SGR 90); British spelling of gray.

bg-black(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:169

Black background (SGR 40).

bg-red(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:171

Red background (SGR 41).

bg-green(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:173

Green background (SGR 42).

bg-yellow(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:175

Yellow background (SGR 43).

bg-blue(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:177

Blue background (SGR 44).

bg-magenta(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:179

Magenta background (SGR 45).

bg-cyan(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:181

Cyan background (SGR 46).

bg-white(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:183

White background (SGR 47).

bg-bright-black(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:190

Bright black background (SGR 100).

bg-bright-red(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:192

Bright red background (SGR 101).

bg-bright-green(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:194

Bright green background (SGR 102).

bg-bright-yellow(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:196

Bright yellow background (SGR 103).

bg-bright-blue(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:198

Bright blue background (SGR 104).

bg-bright-magenta(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:200

Bright magenta background (SGR 105).

bg-bright-cyan(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:202

Bright cyan background (SGR 106).

bg-bright-white(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:204

Bright white background (SGR 107).

fg-256(n, s)
beagle.ansi.bg:212

256-color palette foreground; n is a palette index 0..255: ESC[38;5;n m.

bg-256(n, s)
beagle.ansi.bg:216

256-color palette background; n is a palette index 0..255: ESC[48;5;n m.

rgb(r, g, b, s)
beagle.ansi.bg:220

Truecolor foreground from r,g,b (each 0..255): ESC[38;2;r;g;b m.

bg-rgb(r, g, b, s)
beagle.ansi.bg:224

Truecolor background from r,g,b (each 0..255): ESC[48;2;r;g;b m.

hyperlink(url, label)
beagle.ansi.bg:236

Wrap label in an OSC 8 terminal hyperlink pointing at url: ESC]8;;<url>ESC\<label>ESC]8;;ESC\. Returns label unchanged when disabled.

combine(fns)
beagle.ansi.bg:252

Build one styling function from fns (each string->string), applied left-to-right so combine([bold, red])("x") == bold(red("x")).

sgr-param-code?(c)
beagle.ansi.bg:273

True when byte code c is an SGR parameter character: a digit '0'..'9' or ';'.

strip-ansi(s)
beagle.ansi.bg:283

Remove all SGR escape sequences (ESC[<params>m) from s, returning the plain text; malformed/non-SGR escapes are kept verbatim.

#beagle.bail

11/11 documented
add(a, b)
beagle.bail.bg:23

Tier-2 bail-out for +: adds a and b via the polymorphic path.

sub(a, b)
beagle.bail.bg:25

Tier-2 bail-out for -: subtracts b from a via the polymorphic path.

mul(a, b)
beagle.bail.bg:27

Tier-2 bail-out for *: multiplies a and b via the polymorphic path.

div(a, b)
beagle.bail.bg:29

Tier-2 bail-out for /: divides a by b via the polymorphic path.

modulo(a, b)
beagle.bail.bg:31

Tier-2 bail-out for %: returns a modulo b via the polymorphic path.

lt(a, b)
beagle.bail.bg:34

Tier-2 bail-out for <: returns whether a is less than b.

lte(a, b)
beagle.bail.bg:36

Tier-2 bail-out for <=: returns whether a is less than or equal to b.

gt(a, b)
beagle.bail.bg:38

Tier-2 bail-out for >: returns whether a is greater than b.

gte(a, b)
beagle.bail.bg:40

Tier-2 bail-out for >=: returns whether a is greater than or equal to b.

eq(a, b)
beagle.bail.bg:42

Tier-2 bail-out for ==: returns whether a and b are equal.

ne(a, b)
beagle.bail.bg:44

Tier-2 bail-out for !=: returns whether a and b are not equal.

#beagle.base64

11/11 documented
to-bytes(input)
beagle.base64.bg:28

Normalize input to a Vec<Int> of byte values. A String is converted to its raw UTF-8 bytes (multibyte-correct via a string-builder); a Vec<Int> is assumed to already be byte values and returned unchanged.

encode-with(input, alphabet, pad?)
beagle.base64.bg:54

Core base64 encoder. Encodes input (String or Vec<Int>) using the 64-char alphabet, emitting '=' padding for the trailing 1- or 2-byte group only when pad? is true. Returns the encoded String.

decode-char-value(code)
beagle.base64.bg:103

Map a single character code to its 0..63 base64 value, accepting both the standard ('+', '/') and URL-safe ('-', '_') symbols. Returns -1 for any non-alphabet character (the caller handles padding/whitespace).

decode-any(text)
beagle.base64.bg:138

Core base64 decoder. Decodes text (standard or URL-safe alphabet) into a Vec<Int> of byte values, ignoring '=' padding and ASCII whitespace. Throws if any other invalid base64 character is encountered.

encode(input)
beagle.base64.bg:179

Encode input (String or Vec<Int>) to a standard-alphabet base64 String with '=' padding. Thin wrapper over encode-with using STD_ALPHABET.

decode(text)
beagle.base64.bg:186

Decode a standard base64 String to a Vec<Int> of byte values. Thin wrapper over decode-any (which also tolerates URL-safe symbols and whitespace).

encode-url(input)
beagle.base64.bg:194

Encode input (String or Vec<Int>) to a URL-safe base64 String using '-' and '_' and emitting NO padding. Thin wrapper over encode-with using URL_ALPHABET.

decode-url(text)
beagle.base64.bg:202

Decode a URL-safe base64 String to a Vec<Int> of byte values. Also accepts standard-alphabet input and '=' padding. Thin wrapper over decode-any.

decode-strict(text)
beagle.base64.bg:214

Strictly decode a canonical base64 String to a Vec<Int> of byte values. Throws on malformed input: length not a multiple of 4, any non-alphabet character, or '=' padding that is misplaced or longer than two characters.

decode-to-string(text)
beagle.base64.bg:249

Decode a base64 String and return the decoded bytes as a UTF-8 String. Convenience over decode + bytes-to-string; lenient like decode (tolerates missing padding, ASCII whitespace, and both alphabets).

bytes-to-string(bytes)
beagle.base64.bg:257

Build a String from a Vec<Int> of byte values, appending each as a raw UTF-8 byte. Useful for round-tripping decoded textual data back to a String.

#beagle.bigint

22/26 documented
trim-limbs(limbs)
beagle.bigint.bg:41

Return a new limb vector with the most-significant (trailing) zero limbs removed, so the result has no leading-zero limbs.

make(sign, limbs)
beagle.bigint.bg:59

Construct a canonical BigInt from sign and a possibly-untrimmed limb vector; if the trimmed limbs are empty the result is canonical zero (sign 0).

zero()
beagle.bigint.bg:70

Return the canonical zero BigInt (sign 0, empty limbs).

from-int(n)
beagle.bigint.bg:77

Build a BigInt from a native Int, handling negatives and the full 62-bit range (including the most-negative value, whose magnitude is never formed).

from-string(s)
beagle.bigint.bg:109

Parse a decimal string (optional leading '+'/'-', then one or more digits; leading zeros allowed) into a BigInt. Throws on empty input, a sign with no digits, or any non-digit character.

is-zero?(b)
beagle.bigint.bg:163

Return true if b is the BigInt zero (sign 0).

cmp-mag(a, b)
beagle.bigint.bg:173

Compare two magnitudes (limb vectors), ignoring sign. Returns -1, 0, or 1.

add-mag(a, b)
beagle.bigint.bg:199

Add two magnitudes (limb vectors) and return a trimmed limb vector.

sub-mag(a, b)
beagle.bigint.bg:224

Subtract magnitude b from magnitude a (caller must ensure a >= b) and return a trimmed limb vector.

mul-mag(a, b)
beagle.bigint.bg:249

Multiply two magnitudes with schoolbook long multiplication, returning a trimmed limb vector (empty when either input is empty).

compare(a, b)
beagle.bigint.bg:301

Compare two BigInts by value (sign then magnitude). Returns -1, 0, or 1.

negate(b)
beagle.bigint.bg:326

Return b with its sign flipped; zero (sign 0) is unchanged.

add(a, b)
beagle.bigint.bg:332

Add two BigInts, returning a canonical BigInt.

sub(a, b)
beagle.bigint.bg:361

Subtract b from a; thin wrapper for add(a, negate(b)).

mul(a, b)
beagle.bigint.bg:367

Multiply two BigInts, returning a canonical BigInt (zero if either is zero).

abs(b)
beagle.bigint.bg:439

Absolute value of a BigInt: its magnitude as a non-negative BigInt.

divmod(a, b)
beagle.bigint.bg:451

Truncated division with remainder: returns [quotient, remainder] with a == quotient*b + remainder, the quotient truncated toward zero, and the remainder carrying the sign of a (Java/Rust BigInteger semantics). Throws on division by zero.

div(a, b)
beagle.bigint.bg:463

Truncated quotient a / b (toward zero). Throws on division by zero.

mod(a, b)
beagle.bigint.bg:469

Remainder of a / b, carrying the sign of a (a == div(a,b)*b + mod(a,b)). Throws on division by zero.

pow(base, exp)
beagle.bigint.bg:475

Raise base (a BigInt) to a non-negative integer exp (an Int) via square-and-multiply. Throws on a negative exponent.

append-padded-limb!(builder, limb)
beagle.bigint.bg:503

Append limb (0..9999) to builder as a 4-digit zero-padded group; used for every limb except the most-significant one.

to-string(b)
beagle.bigint.bg:519

Render a BigInt as a decimal string ("0" for zero, leading '-' for negatives).

4 undocumented
  • limb-shift-add(rem, digit)
  • find-quotient-digit(rem, b)
  • divmod-mag(a, b)
  • BigInt_compare-to(a, b)method

#beagle.channel

21/21 documented
make-channel()
beagle.channel.bg:106

Create a new empty, open, unbounded channel.

close!(ch)
beagle.channel.bg:121

Mark ch closed (permanent, idempotent). Returns null. Buffered values can still be drained.

closed?(ch)
beagle.channel.bg:127

True iff ch has been closed via close!. A snapshot; may be stale under concurrency.

send!(ch, v)
beagle.channel.bg:136

Enqueue v at the tail of ch and return v. Lock-free (inlined CAS retry). Throws if ch is closed.

send-loop(ch, v)
beagle.channel.bg:150

Internal CAS retry helper for send!: append v, install, retry on contention. Returns null.

try-receive(ch)
beagle.channel.bg:166

Non-blocking dequeue. Returns {:some value} if an element was removed, {:closed true} if closed and drained, or {:none true} if empty and open.

try-receive-loop(ch)
beagle.channel.bg:171

Internal CAS retry helper for try-receive: read head, install tail (drop index 0), retry on contention.

receive(ch)
beagle.channel.bg:212

Blocking dequeue: spins with growing backoff until a value is available. Returns the value, or the :closed keyword if the channel is closed and drained.

receive-spin(ch, backoff)
beagle.channel.bg:217

Internal spin loop for receive: polls try-receive, backing off (capped at 256) while empty; returns :closed once closed and drained.

spin-backoff(n)
beagle.channel.bg:241

Busy-burn n iterations of pure work (no allocation) to throttle re-polling. Returns null.

channel-count(ch)
beagle.channel.bg:254

Number of buffered (unreceived) elements. A snapshot; may be stale under concurrency.

channel-empty?(ch)
beagle.channel.bg:260

True iff the channel had no buffered elements at the moment we looked.

make-mutex()
beagle.channel.bg:280

Create a new unlocked mutex.

acquire!(mutex)
beagle.channel.bg:286

Acquire the lock, spinning with growing backoff until the CAS false -> true wins. Returns null.

acquire-spin(mutex, backoff)
beagle.channel.bg:291

Internal spin loop for acquire!: retries the CAS, backing off (capped at 256) until acquired.

release!(mutex)
beagle.channel.bg:304

Release the lock by resetting it to false. Only the holder should call this. Returns null.

with-lock(mutex, thunk)
beagle.channel.bg:312

Run thunk while holding mutex, releasing on both normal return and exception (re-thrown). Returns thunk's value.

make-counter()
beagle.channel.bg:335

Create a new counter starting at 0.

incr!(counter)
beagle.channel.bg:342

Atomically add 1 and return the new value. Thin wrapper over add!(counter, 1).

add!(counter, delta)
beagle.channel.bg:348

Atomically add delta and return the new value. Inlined CAS retry loop.

counter-value(counter)
beagle.channel.bg:361

Read the current counter value (snapshot).

#beagle.cli

32/32 documented
make-spec(prog, desc)
beagle.cli.bg:46

Create an empty spec with a program name and description.

add-flag(spec, name, short, help)
beagle.cli.bg:55

Add a boolean flag to the spec. short may be null for no short form.

add-option(spec, name, short, help, required, default)
beagle.cli.bg:62

Add a value-taking option to the spec. short may be null. required is a bool. default is used when absent.

add-positional(spec, name, help, required)
beagle.cli.bg:72

Add a positional argument to the spec.

find-flag-by-name(spec, name)
beagle.cli.bg:83

Find a flag entry by its long :name. Returns the entry map or null.

find-option-by-name(spec, name)
beagle.cli.bg:89

Find an option entry by its long :name. Returns the entry map or null.

find-flag-by-short(spec, short)
beagle.cli.bg:95

Find a flag entry by its single-character :short name. Returns entry or null.

find-option-by-short(spec, short)
beagle.cli.bg:101

Find an option entry by its single-character :short name. Returns entry or null.

seed-defaults(spec)
beagle.cli.bg:113

Build the initial options map: each flag and :help default to false, each option to its declared :default (or null). Returns the seeded map.

st-new(spec)
beagle.cli.bg:129

Create a fresh parse-state atom holding {:options seeded :positionals [] :errors []}.

st-set-option(state, name, value)
beagle.cli.bg:136

Set name to value in the state atom's :options map (mutates via swap!).

st-add-positional(state, value)
beagle.cli.bg:143

Append value to the state atom's :positionals vector (mutates via swap!).

st-add-error(state, msg)
beagle.cli.bg:150

Append error string msg to the state atom's :errors vector (mutates via swap!).

parse(argv, spec)
beagle.cli.bg:158

Parse argv (a vector of strings) against a spec. Returns {:options map :positionals vector :errors vector}.

is-long-arg(arg)
beagle.cli.bg:191

True if arg is a long option: begins with "--" and is longer than 2 chars.

is-short-arg(arg)
beagle.cli.bg:199

True if arg is a short option: starts with a single "-", longer than 1, not "--", and not a negative number (char after "-" is not a digit).

parse-long(state, spec, argv, arg, i, n)
beagle.cli.bg:220

Handle a "--..." token (flag, --help, --name=value, or --name value option), updating state and returning the next argv index to read.

apply-long-option(state, spec, name, value)
beagle.cli.bg:263

Apply a "--name=value": set the option if name is an option; if it is a flag, record an error (flags take no value) but still mark it true; else record an unknown-option error.

parse-short(state, spec, argv, arg, i, n)
beagle.cli.bg:282

Handle a "-..." token (single short flag/option or a cluster), updating state and returning the next argv index to read.

parse-short-cluster(state, spec, argv, cluster, i, n)
beagle.cli.bg:299

Walk each char of a short cluster: flag chars set their flag true; an option char consumes the rest of the cluster (-ovalue) or the next argv token (-o value). Records errors for unknown chars / missing values. Returns next index.

finalize(state, spec)
beagle.cli.bg:349

Finish parsing: if --help was given, skip validation; otherwise check required options and positionals. Returns the dereferenced result map.

check-required-options(state, spec)
beagle.cli.bg:363

Record a "missing required option" error for each required option left null. Returns null.

check-required-positionals(state, spec)
beagle.cli.bg:378

Record a "missing required positional" error for each required positional not supplied (by position). Returns null.

get-int(parsed, name)
beagle.cli.bg:405

Read option name from a parsed result, coercing its value to an Int. Returns null when the option is absent (its stored value is null). Throws a clear error when the value is present but is not a valid integer.

get-float(parsed, name)
beagle.cli.bg:422

Read option name from a parsed result, coercing its value to a Float. Returns null when the option is absent (its stored value is null). Throws a clear error when the value is present but is not a valid float.

get-bool(parsed, name)
beagle.cli.bg:440

Read option name from a parsed result, coercing its value to a Bool. A flag stored as true/false is returned as-is; a string option value must be "true" or "false". Returns null when the option is absent (its stored value is null). Throws a clear error on any other present value.

get-or(parsed, name, default)
beagle.cli.bg:467

Read option name from a parsed result, returning default when the option is absent (its stored value is null) and the stored value otherwise.

help-text(spec)
beagle.cli.bg:481

Build a usage / help string from a spec.

build-usage-line(spec)
beagle.cli.bg:494

Build the "Usage: prog [options] <required> [optional]" line from the spec.

build-positionals-section(spec)
beagle.cli.bg:515

Build the "Arguments:" help section listing positionals, or "" if none.

build-options-section(spec)
beagle.cli.bg:528

Build the "Options:" help section listing each option (short/long, help, and a "(required)" marker), or "" if there are no options.

build-flags-section(spec)
beagle.cli.bg:549

Build the "Flags:" help section listing each flag plus the always-present "-h, --help" line.

#beagle.containers

34/49 documented
deque-new()
beagle.containers.bg:35

Create a new empty deque.

deque-count(d)
beagle.containers.bg:40

Number of elements in the deque.

deque-empty?(d)
beagle.containers.bg:45

Is the deque empty?

coll-reverse(v)
beagle.containers.bg:51

Return a new vector containing the elements of v in reverse order.

coll-subvec(v, start, end)
beagle.containers.bg:64

Return a new vector with the elements of v from index start (inclusive) to end (exclusive).

push-front(d, value)
beagle.containers.bg:75

Push a value onto the FRONT of the deque, returning a new deque.

push-back(d, value)
beagle.containers.bg:80

Push a value onto the BACK of the deque, returning a new deque.

deque-rebalance(d)
beagle.containers.bg:91

Rebalance a deque when one side is empty: move half of the non-empty side across (reversing as needed to preserve logical order), returning a new deque. Returns d unchanged when neither side needs rebalancing.

pop-front(d)
beagle.containers.bg:120

Pop from the FRONT. Returns [value, new-deque]. Throws on an empty deque.

pop-back(d)
beagle.containers.bg:148

Pop from the BACK. Returns [value, new-deque]. Throws on an empty deque.

deque-peek-front(d)
beagle.containers.bg:177

Peek at the FRONT (logical head) element WITHOUT removing it. Throws on an empty deque (matching pop-front's error style).

deque-peek-back(d)
beagle.containers.bg:194

Peek at the BACK (logical tail) element WITHOUT removing it. Throws on an empty deque (matching pop-back's error style).

deque-to-vec(d)
beagle.containers.bg:210

Materialize the deque into a plain vector in logical (head->tail) order.

dm-new(default)
beagle.containers.bg:228

Create a new default-map with the given default value.

dm-get(dm, key)
beagle.containers.bg:236

Look up a key, returning the default if the key is absent.

Uses contains? (not v == null) so a key explicitly stored with a null value reads back as null, distinct from an absent key returning the default.

dm-assoc(dm, key, value)
beagle.containers.bg:245

Associate key->value, returning a new default-map.

dm-update(dm, key, f)
beagle.containers.bg:251

Update key by applying f to its current value (or the default if absent), returning a new default-map.

dm-count(dm)
beagle.containers.bg:257

Number of explicitly-stored keys.

dm-remove(dm, key)
beagle.containers.bg:264

Remove key from a default-map, returning a new default-map whose table no longer contains key. The default value is preserved. Removing a key that is absent yields an equivalent default-map (no error).

om-new()
beagle.containers.bg:282

Create a new empty ordered map.

om-get(om, key)
beagle.containers.bg:287

Look up a key (null if absent).

om-has-key?(om, key)
beagle.containers.bg:294

Return true if key is present in the ordered map's insertion-order vector (linear scan using structural equality).

om-assoc(om, key, value)
beagle.containers.bg:302

Associate key->value, returning a new ordered map. New keys are appended to the insertion order; existing keys keep their position.

om-update(om, key, f)
beagle.containers.bg:314

Update key by applying f to its current value (or null if the key is absent), returning a new ordered map. Insertion order is preserved: an existing key keeps its position, a new key is appended.

om-keys-in-order(om)
beagle.containers.bg:319

The keys in insertion order (a vector).

om-pairs(om)
beagle.containers.bg:324

The [key, value] pairs in insertion order (a vector of 2-vectors).

om-vals-in-order(om)
beagle.containers.bg:337

The values in insertion order (a vector).

om-count(om)
beagle.containers.bg:349

Number of entries.

om-remove(om, key)
beagle.containers.bg:357

Remove key from an ordered map, returning a new ordered map with key removed from BOTH the insertion-order vector and the backing table. The relative order of the remaining keys is preserved. Removing a key that is absent yields an equivalent ordered map (no error).

om-dissoc(om, key)
beagle.containers.bg:367

Alias for om-remove: remove key from an ordered map (Clojure-style dissoc name). Returns a new ordered map with key gone from both the order vector and the table.

vec-member?(v, x)
beagle.containers.bg:381

Return true if x appears in vector v (linear scan using structural equality).

set-union-vec(a, b)
beagle.containers.bg:395

Union of two vectors-as-sets: all distinct elements from both.

set-intersection-vec(a, b)
beagle.containers.bg:400

Intersection: distinct elements of a that also appear in b.

set-difference-vec(a, b)
beagle.containers.bg:416

Difference: distinct elements of a that do NOT appear in b.

15 undocumented
  • Deque_length(d)method
  • Deque_seq(d)method
  • Deque_conj(d, x)method
  • DefaultMap_get(dm, key)DefaultMap_get(dm, key, default)methodarities 2, 3
  • DefaultMap_assoc(dm, key, value)method
  • DefaultMap_length(dm)method
  • DefaultMap_contains?(dm, key)method
  • DefaultMap_keys(dm)method
  • DefaultMap_seq(dm)method
  • OrderedMap_get(om, key)OrderedMap_get(om, key, default)methodarities 2, 3
  • OrderedMap_assoc(om, key, value)method
  • OrderedMap_length(om)method
  • OrderedMap_contains?(om, key)method
  • OrderedMap_keys(om)method
  • OrderedMap_seq(om)method

#beagle.csv

13/13 documented
parse-delim(text, delim, quote)
beagle.csv.bg:37

Parse delimiter-separated text into a Vec of rows of string fields, using the byte codes delim and quote (e.g. from char-code) for the field separator and the quote character. The shared core of parse/parse-with.

parse(text)
beagle.csv.bg:114

Parse RFC 4180 CSV text into a Vec of rows, each a Vec of string fields. Handles quoted fields containing commas, embedded newlines, and doubled quotes. Empty input yields []; a trailing terminator adds no empty row.

opt-char(opts, key, default-str)
beagle.csv.bg:123

Read a one-character option string from map opts at key, defaulting to default-str; throws if the resolved value is not exactly one character.

parse-with(text, opts)
beagle.csv.bg:136

Parse delimiter-separated text using an options map: :delimiter and :quote are one-character strings (defaulting to "," and "\""). Returns a Vec of rows of string fields. Throws if an option is not one character or if the delimiter and quote characters are equal.

parse-tsv(text)
beagle.csv.bg:148

Parse tab-separated (TSV) text into a Vec of rows of string fields, using the tab character as the delimiter and the double-quote for quoting.

parse-with-header(text)
beagle.csv.bg:158

Parse CSV whose first row is a header into a Vec of maps keyed by the header names. Extra fields are dropped; missing fields default to "". Returns [] when the input has no rows.

needs-quoting?(s)
beagle.csv.bg:191

Return true if string s must be quoted per RFC 4180, i.e. it contains a comma, double-quote, CR, or LF.

write-field!(builder, s)
beagle.csv.bg:209

Append field string s to string-builder builder, wrapping it in quotes and doubling embedded quotes only when needs-quoting? is true. Mutates the builder; returns null.

write-row!(builder, row)
beagle.csv.bg:236

Append row (a Vec of fields) to builder, comma-separating fields (each via to-string + write-field!) and terminating with CRLF. Mutates the builder; returns null.

write(rows)
beagle.csv.bg:255

Serialize a Vec of rows (each a Vec of fields) into a CSV string with CRLF line endings, including a trailing CRLF.

write-with-header(header, rows)
beagle.csv.bg:268

Serialize header as the first CSV row followed by rows (each a Vec of fields), returning a CSV string with CRLF line endings.

write-row(fields)
beagle.csv.bg:288

Serialize one row (fields, a Vec of fields) into a single CSV line string, comma-separating fields and quoting/escaping per RFC 4180. Adds no trailing terminator. An empty Vec yields "".

write-rows(rows)
beagle.csv.bg:310

Serialize rows (each a Vec of fields) into a CSV string, joining rows with CRLF and adding no trailing terminator. Each row is rendered via write-row (RFC 4180 quoting). An empty Vec yields "".

#beagle.date

19/20 documented
from-epoch(secs)
beagle.date.bg:34

Convert epoch seconds (UTC) into a DateTime using beagle.time/civil-from-epoch.

now()
beagle.date.bg:48

Return the current wall-clock time as a UTC DateTime.

to-epoch(dt)
beagle.date.bg:54

Convert a DateTime back to epoch seconds (UTC); inverse of from-epoch.

is-leap-year?(y)
beagle.date.bg:76

Return true if year y is a leap year under the proleptic Gregorian rule.

day-of-year(dt)
beagle.date.bg:94

Ordinal day-of-year for a DateTime: 1 for January 1st, up to 365 (or 366 in a leap year) for December 31st. Computed purely from the year/month/day fields by differencing day counts since the civil epoch, so it inherits the module's leap-year handling and ignores time-of-day.

weekday(dt)
beagle.date.bg:104

Day of week for a DateTime as an integer, 0 = Sunday .. 6 = Saturday.

weekday-name(dt)
beagle.date.bg:111

Full English weekday name for a DateTime, e.g. "Monday". Built on top of weekday (0 = Sunday .. 6 = Saturday), so the name always matches that numbering.

is-weekend?(dt)
beagle.date.bg:118

True when a DateTime falls on a Saturday or Sunday. Built on top of weekday (0 = Sunday .. 6 = Saturday), so it matches that numbering.

add-seconds(dt, n)
beagle.date.bg:128

Return a new DateTime n seconds after dt (negative n moves backward).

add-days(dt, n)
beagle.date.bg:133

Return a new DateTime n days after dt; thin wrapper over add-seconds.

diff-seconds(a, b)
beagle.date.bg:139

Whole seconds between two DateTimes, computed as b - a (signed).

diff-days(a, b)
beagle.date.bg:147

Whole calendar days from a to b (signed), comparing only the UTC date fields (year/month/day) — time-of-day is ignored. Counts day numbers since the epoch, so it handles month and year rollover and is negative when b precedes a. For example, 2024-01-15 -> 2024-01-20 is 5, the reverse is -5.

pad-num(n, width)
beagle.date.bg:161

Format integer n as a zero-padded decimal string at least width digits wide. Assumes n >= 0; does not truncate values wider than width.

format-iso8601(dt)
beagle.date.bg:175

Render a DateTime as an ISO-8601 UTC string "YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SSZ" (always with the trailing 'Z'). Negative years emit a leading '-' with 4-digit magnitude.

days-in-month(y, month)
beagle.date.bg:209

Number of days in month (1-12) for year y, accounting for leap Februaries.

parse-digits(s, start, width)
beagle.date.bg:224

Parse width consecutive ASCII digits of s starting at start into a non-negative integer; throws a parse-iso8601 error on any non-digit.

expect-sep(s, idx, expected)
beagle.date.bg:243

Assert the character of s at idx equals the single-char expected, throwing a parse-iso8601 separator error otherwise; returns null on success.

parse-iso8601(s)
beagle.date.bg:255

Strictly parse an ISO-8601 UTC string "YYYY-MM-DDTHH:MM:SS" with an optional trailing 'Z' into a DateTime. Validates separator positions and field ranges, throwing a "beagle.date/parse-iso8601: ..." error on any malformed input.

dt-equal?(a, b)
beagle.date.bg:332

True when two DateTimes have identical year/month/day/hour/minute/second (field-wise comparison, since structs don't compare with ==).

1 undocumented
  • DateTime_compare-to(a, b)method

#beagle.effect

1/2 documented
resume-tail(resume, value)
beagle.effect.bg:62

Resume a captured effect continuation in tail position with value, avoiding stack growth from nested resumes. Delegates to builtin/resume-tail.

1 undocumented
  • handle(self, op, resume)

#beagle.ffi

47/50 documented
load-library(path)
builtin

Load a dynamic library (shared object) from the given path.

Returns a Library struct that can be used with get-function.

Examples: (let lib (ffi/load-library "libm.dylib"))

get-function(library, name, arg_types, return_type)
builtin

Get a function from a loaded library.

arg_types is an array of Type values, return_type is a Type.

Examples: (let sqrt-fn (ffi/get-function lib "sqrt" [Type.F64] Type.F64))

get-symbol(library, name)
builtin

Get a raw function pointer (symbol) from a loaded library.

Returns a Pointer struct. Used with call-variadic for per-call type specification.

create-callback(fn, arg_types, return_type)
builtin

Create an FFI callback from a Beagle function.

Returns a Pointer that can be passed to C functions expecting function pointers. arg_types is an array of Type values for the C callback parameters. return_type is the C return type.

Callbacks must be called from the main thread.

allocate(size)
builtin

Allocate size bytes of unmanaged memory.

Returns a Pointer. Must be freed with deallocate.

deallocate(ptr)
builtin

Free memory allocated with allocate.

get-u32(ptr, offset)
builtin

Read an unsigned 32-bit integer from memory at ptr + offset.

set-i16(ptr, offset, value)
builtin

Write a signed 16-bit integer to memory at ptr + offset.

set-i32(ptr, offset, value)
builtin

Write a signed 32-bit integer to memory at ptr + offset.

set-u8(ptr, offset, value)
builtin

Write an unsigned 8-bit integer (byte) to memory at ptr + offset.

get-u8(ptr, offset)
builtin

Read an unsigned 8-bit integer (byte) from memory at ptr + offset.

get-i32(ptr, offset)
builtin

Read a signed 32-bit integer from memory at ptr + offset.

set-i8(ptr, offset, value)
builtin

Write a signed 8-bit integer to memory at ptr + offset.

get-i8(ptr, offset)
builtin

Read a signed 8-bit integer from memory at ptr + offset.

set-u16(ptr, offset, value)
builtin

Write an unsigned 16-bit integer to memory at ptr + offset.

get-u16(ptr, offset)
builtin

Read an unsigned 16-bit integer from memory at ptr + offset.

get-i16(ptr, offset)
builtin

Read a signed 16-bit integer from memory at ptr + offset.

set-u32(ptr, offset, value)
builtin

Write an unsigned 32-bit integer to memory at ptr + offset.

set-i64(ptr, offset, value)
builtin

Write a signed 64-bit integer to memory at ptr + offset.

get-i64(ptr, offset)
builtin

Read a signed 64-bit integer from memory at ptr + offset.

set-u64(ptr, offset, value)
builtin

Write an unsigned 64-bit integer to memory at ptr + offset.

get-u64(ptr, offset)
builtin

Read an unsigned 64-bit integer from memory at ptr + offset.

set-f32(ptr, offset, value)
builtin

Write a 32-bit float to memory at ptr + offset. Accepts Int or Float.

set-f64(ptr, offset, value)
builtin

Write a 64-bit float to memory at ptr + offset. Accepts Int or Float.

get-f32(ptr, offset)
builtin

Read a 32-bit float from memory at ptr + offset. Returns a Beagle Float.

get-f64(ptr, offset)
builtin

Read a 64-bit float from memory at ptr + offset. Returns a Beagle Float.

get-string(buffer, offset, length)
builtin

Read a string from a buffer at the given offset with the specified length.

Examples: (ffi/get-string buf 0 10) ; Read 10 bytes starting at offset 0

get-string-and-free(buffer, offset, length)
builtin

Read a string from a buffer and free the native buffer. Combines get-string + deallocate to avoid holding Buffer reference across GC.

create-array(size)
builtin

Create a new FFI buffer/array of the given size in bytes.

copy-bytes(src, src_offset, dest, dest_offset, length)
builtin

Copy length bytes from src+src_offset to dest+dest_offset.

realloc(ptr, new_size)
builtin

Reallocate memory to a new size, preserving contents.

buffer-size(buffer)
builtin

Get the size of a buffer in bytes.

write-buffer-offset(buffer, offset, value, size)
builtin

Write a value to a buffer at a given offset.

translate-bytes(buffer, offset, length, table)
builtin

Translate bytes using a lookup table.

reverse-bytes(buffer, offset, length)
builtin

Reverse bytes in place in a buffer.

find-byte(buffer, offset, length, byte)
builtin

Find the first occurrence of a byte in a buffer.

Returns the offset or -1 if not found.

call-variadic(func_ptr, args, types, return_type)
beagle.ffi.bg:117

Call raw function pointer func_ptr with args whose per-argument FFI types are given in types, returning a value of return_type. Registers the C call with the GC for the duration.

size-of(ty)
beagle.ffi.bg:128

Return the byte size of a primitive FFI ty. Throws for String, Void, and Structure, which have no fixed primitive size.

read-at(ptr, offset, ty)
beagle.ffi.bg:152

Read a value of type ty from ptr at byte offset, dispatching to the matching get-* builtin. Throws for Pointer/MutablePointer/String/Void/ Structure, which are not supported here.

write-at(ptr, offset, ty, value)
beagle.ffi.bg:176

Write value of type ty to ptr at byte offset, dispatching to the matching set-* builtin. Throws for Pointer/MutablePointer/String/Void/ Structure, which are not supported here.

cell(ty, initial)
beagle.ffi.bg:208

Allocate a GC-managed typed Cell of type ty initialized to initial. Its raw address can be passed directly to C; the backing memory is freed when the Cell is collected.

cell-get(c)
beagle.ffi.bg:219

Read the current value out of cell c, using the cell's stored type.

cell-set(c, value)
beagle.ffi.bg:224

Store value into cell c, using the cell's stored type.

array(ty, length)
beagle.ffi.bg:234

Allocate a GC-managed TypedArray of length elements of type ty. The raw address can be passed directly to C; the backing memory is freed when the array is collected.

array-get(a, i)
beagle.ffi.bg:245

Read element i from typed array a (offset = i * element stride).

array-set(a, i, value)
beagle.ffi.bg:250

Write value to element i of typed array a (offset = i * stride).

forget(x)
beagle.ffi.bg:261

Disown the off-heap memory held by finalizable FFI struct x (Buffer, Cell, or TypedArray), handing ownership to C. Afterward the GC finalizer no-ops and beagle-side reads/writes raise a resumable FFIError.

3 undocumented
  • call-ffi-info()builtin
  • call-variadic-raw()builtin
  • copy-bytes_filter()builtin

#beagle.glob

15/15 documented
code-at(s, i)
beagle.glob.bg:30

Return the integer character code of s[i]. Thin wrapper over char-code.

is-slash-code(c)
beagle.glob.bg:36

True if char code c is the path separator '/' (code 47).

class-match(pattern, open-idx, target-code)
beagle.glob.bg:54

Match a character class beginning just past '[' (at open-idx) against the char code target-code. Handles ranges (a-z) and negation (!/^). Returns [matched?, next-index] where next-index is just past the ']'; an unterminated class returns [false, -1]. Classes never match '/'.

is-double-star(pattern, pi)
beagle.glob.bg:127

True if the '*' run at index pi is a cross-slash ** wildcard, i.e. a whole path segment (preceded by start-of-pattern or '/', followed by end-of-pattern or '/'). Otherwise the run collapses to a single '*'.

match-from(pattern, pi, name, ni)
beagle.glob.bg:157

Core recursive glob matcher: does pattern from index pi match name from index ni? Handles *, **, ?, [...] classes, and \ escapes with explicit star backtracking. Returns Bool.

match?(pattern, name)
beagle.glob.bg:301

Match a glob pattern against a name (or path).

Examples: match?("*.txt", "foo.txt") // => true match?("*.txt", "foo.md") // => false match?("a?c", "abc") // => true match?("[a-c]x", "bx") // => true match?("src/**/*.bg", "src/a/b/c.bg") // => true match?("*", "a/b") // => false

translate(pattern)
beagle.glob.bg:324

Translate a glob pattern into a space-separated token description.

Useful for verifying how a pattern parses.

Examples: translate("a*c") // => "LIT(a) STAR LIT(c)" translate("src/**/*.bg") // => "LIT(s) LIT(r) LIT(c) LIT(/) DOUBLESTAR LIT(/) STAR LIT(.) LIT(b) LIT(g)"

translate-class(pattern, pi)
beagle.glob.bg:382

Render the character-class token starting at '[' (pattern[pi]) for translate. Returns [token-string, next-index]; an unterminated class yields ["LIT([)", pi + 1].

match-ci?(pattern, name)
beagle.glob.bg:431

Case-insensitive variant of match?: matches pattern against name ignoring ASCII letter case. Lowercases both sides, then runs the same matcher (so *, ?, classes, ranges, and escapes behave identically).

Examples: match-ci?("*.TXT", "foo.txt") // => true match-ci?("[A-C]x", "BX") // => true

brace-first-open(pattern)
beagle.glob.bg:450

Index of the first unescaped '{' in pattern, or -1 if none.

brace-matching-close(pattern, open)
beagle.glob.bg:472

Index of the '}' matching the '{' at open in pattern, accounting for nested braces and '\' escapes. Returns -1 if the group is never closed.

brace-split-commas(inner)
beagle.glob.bg:505

Split inner (the text between a group's braces) on its depth-0 commas, honoring nested {...} and '\' escapes. Returns a vector of option strings; text with no top-level comma yields a single-element vector.

expand-braces(pattern)
beagle.glob.bg:559

Expand shell-style brace groups in pattern into the list of concrete strings they denote, e.g. "a{b,c}d" => ["abd", "acd"]. Multiple groups produce the cartesian product; nested groups expand out-in. A group with no top-level comma, and any unbalanced '{', are left literal. Never throws on a well-formed string; always returns a non-empty vector.

Examples: expand-braces("a{b,c}d") // => ["abd", "acd"] expand-braces("{a,b}{1,2}") // => ["a1", "a2", "b1", "b2"] expand-braces("plain") // => ["plain"]

find-walk(root, start-rel, pattern, acc)
beagle.glob.bg:625

Iteratively walk the directory tree under root (starting at relative path start-rel), accumulating into acc every entry whose path relative to root matches pattern. Uses an explicit worklist (no Beagle recursion across the fs perform/resume boundary); read/stat errors are skipped. Returns the vector of matching relative paths.

find(pattern, dir)
beagle.glob.bg:662

Recursively find paths under dir matching pattern.

Paths are returned relative to dir. Wraps the directory walk in a blocking fs handler, so no handler setup is required by the caller. Pure matching is done by match?; this only walks the directory tree.

Examples: find("src/**/*.bg", ".") // => ["src/a/b.bg", ...]

#beagle.hash

45/45 documented
to-bytes(input)
beagle.hash.bg:22

Normalizes hash input into a Vec<Int> of byte values (0..255). A String is converted to its UTF-8 bytes; a Vec<Int> has each element masked to a byte.

add32(a, b)
beagle.hash.bg:54

Adds two 32-bit words modulo 2^32 (result masked to 32 bits).

rotl32(x, n)
beagle.hash.bg:59

Rotates the 32-bit word x left by n bits.

rotr32(x, n)
beagle.hash.bg:64

Rotates the 32-bit word x right by n bits.

append-hex32!(builder, word)
beagle.hash.bg:72

Appends word to builder as 8 lowercase big-endian hex digits. Returns the builder.

append-hex8!(builder, byte)
beagle.hash.bg:83

Appends byte to builder as 2 lowercase hex digits. Returns the builder.

pad-message(bytes)
beagle.hash.bg:96

Applies SHA-1/SHA-256 (big-endian) message padding to bytes, returning a new Vec<Int> whose length is a multiple of 64.

word-be(bytes, i)
beagle.hash.bg:124

Reads a big-endian 32-bit word from bytes at index i.

word-le(bytes, i)
beagle.hash.bg:130

Reads a little-endian 32-bit word from bytes at index i (used by MD5).

pad-message-le(bytes)
beagle.hash.bg:145

MD5 message padding: like pad-message but with a little-endian 64-bit bit-length field.

md5-s()
beagle.hash.bg:169

Returns the 64 per-round left-rotate amounts for MD5.

md5-k()
beagle.hash.bg:180

Returns the 64 MD5 round constants K[i] = floor(abs(sin(i+1)) * 2^32).

md5-digest-words(raw-bytes)
beagle.hash.bg:207

Runs the MD5 compression over raw-bytes, returning the digest as a Vec of 4 words.

append-hex32-le!(builder, word)
beagle.hash.bg:275

Appends word to builder byte-reversed (little-endian) as 8 hex digits. Returns the builder.

md5(input)
beagle.hash.bg:284

Computes the MD5 hash of input (String or Vec<Int>), returning a 32-char lowercase hex String.

md5-digest-bytes(raw-bytes)
beagle.hash.bg:297

MD5 digest of raw-bytes as a Vec of 16 bytes (little-endian per word), for HMAC.

sha1(input)
beagle.hash.bg:317

Computes the SHA-1 hash of input (String or Vec<Int>), returning a 40-char lowercase hex String.

sha256-digest-words(raw-bytes)
beagle.hash.bg:405

Runs SHA-256 over a Vec<Int> of bytes, returning the digest as a Vec of 8 words.

words-to-hex(words)
beagle.hash.bg:485

Concatenates a Vec of 32-bit words into a big-endian lowercase hex String.

sha256(input)
beagle.hash.bg:496

Computes the SHA-256 hash of input (String or Vec<Int>), returning a 64-char lowercase hex String.

sha256-k()
beagle.hash.bg:502

Returns the 64 SHA-256 round constants.

w64(hi, lo)
beagle.hash.bg:549

Builds a W64 from hi and lo, masking each to 32 bits.

xor64(a, b)
beagle.hash.bg:554

Bitwise XOR of two W64 values.

and64(a, b)
beagle.hash.bg:559

Bitwise AND of two W64 values.

not64(a)
beagle.hash.bg:564

Bitwise NOT (complement) of a W64 value.

add64(a, b)
beagle.hash.bg:569

Adds two W64 values modulo 2^64, carrying the low-half overflow into the high half.

shr64(x, n)
beagle.hash.bg:578

Logical right shift of a W64 by n bits (0 <= n < 64).

shl64(x, n)
beagle.hash.bg:597

Left shift of a W64 by n bits (0 <= n < 64).

rotr64(x, n)
beagle.hash.bg:614

Rotates a W64 right by n bits (0 <= n < 64).

word-be64(bytes, i)
beagle.hash.bg:620

Reads a big-endian 64-bit word from bytes at index i as a W64.

pad-message-128(bytes)
beagle.hash.bg:630

SHA-512 message padding: block size 128 with a 128-bit big-endian length field (the high 64 bits are always zero for our inputs).

sha512-k()
beagle.hash.bg:660

Returns the 80 SHA-512 round constants as W64 values.

sha512-digest-words(raw-bytes)
beagle.hash.bg:707

Runs SHA-512 over a Vec<Int> of bytes, returning the digest as a Vec of 8 W64.

words64-to-hex(words)
beagle.hash.bg:786

Concatenates a Vec of W64 into a big-endian lowercase hex String.

sha512(input)
beagle.hash.bg:798

Computes the SHA-512 hash of input (String or Vec<Int>), returning a 128-char lowercase hex String.

sha512-digest-bytes(raw-bytes)
beagle.hash.bg:804

SHA-512 digest of raw-bytes as a Vec of 64 bytes (big-endian), for HMAC.

words-to-bytes(words)
beagle.hash.bg:830

Converts 8 SHA-256 words into a Vec of 32 bytes (big-endian).

concat-bytes(a, b)
beagle.hash.bg:845

Returns a new byte Vec with the bytes of b appended to a.

bytes-to-hex(bytes)
beagle.hash.bg:857

Returns the lowercase hex String of an arbitrary byte Vec.

hmac-bytes(digest-bytes, block-size, key, msg)
beagle.hash.bg:873

Generic HMAC over a raw-bytes digest function. digest-bytes maps Vec<Int> -> Vec<Int> (the raw hash output); block-size is the hash's internal block size in bytes. Returns the raw HMAC bytes.

hmac-sha256(key, msg)
beagle.hash.bg:897

Computes HMAC-SHA-256 of msg under key, returning a 64-char lowercase hex String.

hmac-md5(key, msg)
beagle.hash.bg:926

Computes HMAC-MD5 of msg under key, returning a 32-char lowercase hex String.

hmac-sha512(key, msg)
beagle.hash.bg:932

Computes HMAC-SHA-512 of msg under key, returning a 128-char lowercase hex String.

crc32(input)
beagle.hash.bg:944

Computes the standard CRC-32 (IEEE 802.3, reflected polynomial 0xEDB88320) of input (a String, hashed as its UTF-8 bytes, or a Vec<Int> of byte values), returning the unsigned 32-bit checksum as an Int. crc32("") == 0.

fnv1a-32(input)
beagle.hash.bg:969

Computes the 32-bit FNV-1a hash of input (a String, hashed as its UTF-8 bytes, or a Vec<Int> of byte values), returning the unsigned 32-bit hash as an Int. Offset basis 2166136261, prime 16777619: for each byte, XOR into the hash then multiply by the prime (masked to 32 bits). fnv1a-32("") == 2166136261.

#beagle.hex

5/5 documented
to-bytes(input)
beagle.hex.bg:19

Coerce input to a Vec<Int> of bytes: a String becomes its UTF-8 bytes (via a string-builder), any other value (a Vec<Int>) is returned unchanged.

nibble-to-code(n)
beagle.hex.bg:38

Map a nibble n (0..15) to its lowercase hex character code ('0'-'9', 'a'-'f').

code-to-nibble(c)
beagle.hex.bg:49

Map a hex digit character code c to its value (0..15), case-insensitive; returns -1 if c is not a hex digit.

encode(input)
beagle.hex.bg:69

Encode input (a Vec<Int> of byte values, or a String by its UTF-8 bytes) as a lowercase hex string with exactly two characters per byte.

decode(s)
beagle.hex.bg:89

Decode hex string s into a Vec<Int> of byte values (case-insensitive). Throws on an odd-length string or any non-hex character.

#beagle.http

56/64 documented
reason-phrase(status)
beagle.http.bg:118

Return the reason phrase for an HTTP status code, or "Unknown" if the code is not in the table.

header(req-or-resp, name)
beagle.http.bg:130

Read a header from a Request or Response by name, case-insensitively; returns null if absent.

set-header(headers, name, value)
beagle.http.bg:136

Return a new headers map with name (lowercased) set to value.

has-header?(headers, name)
beagle.http.bg:142

True if the headers map contains the given header name (case-insensitive).

make-response(status, headers, body)
beagle.http.bg:154

Build a Response from a status code, headers map, and body; the reason phrase is filled in from the status table.

ok(body)
beagle.http.bg:165

200 OK response with a text/plain UTF-8 body.

ok-with(content-type, body)
beagle.http.bg:171

200 OK response with an explicit content type and body.

html(body)
beagle.http.bg:177

200 OK response with a text/html UTF-8 body.

json(json-string)
beagle.http.bg:185

200 OK response with an application/json body; json-string must already be a serialized JSON string.

not-found(body)
beagle.http.bg:191

404 Not Found response with a text/plain body.

status-response(status, body)
beagle.http.bg:197

Response with an arbitrary status code and a text/plain body.

redirect(location)
beagle.http.bg:203

302 Found redirect response to the given location.

with-status(resp, status)
beagle.http.bg:215

Return a copy of resp with its status code (and matching reason phrase) set.

Examples: with-status(ok("x"), 201) // => 201 Created

with-header(resp, name, value)
beagle.http.bg:229

Return a copy of resp with the header set (case-insensitive; replaces any existing value).

Examples: with-header(ok("x"), "X-Request-Id", "abc")

with-body(resp, body)
beagle.http.bg:239

Return a copy of resp with its body replaced.

with-content-type(resp, content-type)
beagle.http.bg:249

Return a copy of resp with its Content-Type header set.

conn-buf(conn)
beagle.http.bg:281

Wrap a raw socket connection in a fresh ConnBuf with an empty leftover buffer.

conn-buf-tls(conn)
beagle.http.bg:287

Wrap a TLS connection handle in a fresh ConnBuf for HTTPS reads.

byte-slice(s, start, end)
beagle.http.bg:294

Return the substring of s between BYTE indices [start, end); "" if end <= start.

cb-fill!(cb)
beagle.http.bg:308

Pull one more socket chunk into the buffer's leftover; returns true if any bytes were appended, false at EOF.

read-line(cb)
beagle.http.bg:328

Read one CRLF/LF-terminated line (terminator stripped); returns null at EOF before any bytes. Over-read bytes stay buffered for the next read.

strip-cr(line)
beagle.http.bg:362

Drop a single trailing CR (the "\r" of a CRLF terminator) from a line.

read-n(cb, n)
beagle.http.bg:377

Read exactly n bytes (or fewer at EOF) from the connection as a string, measured in BYTES; over-read bytes stay buffered.

parse-request-line(line)
beagle.http.bg:411

Parse a request line "METHOD PATH?QUERY VERSION" into [method, path, query, version], or null if malformed.

parse-header-line(line)
beagle.http.bg:436

Parse a "Name: value" header line into [lowercase-name, trimmed-value], or null if there is no colon.

read-headers(cb)
beagle.http.bg:451

Read header lines into a PersistentMap (keys lowercased) until the blank line that ends the header block, or EOF.

parse-chunk-size(line)
beagle.http.bg:493

Parse a chunked-encoding chunk-size line (hex, optionally with ";extension") into an Int.

decode-chunked(s)
beagle.http.bg:516

Decode a complete chunked-transfer-encoding body string into the actual body.

decode-chunked-into(s, pos, out)
beagle.http.bg:523

Internal recursive worker for decode-chunked.

read-chunked-body(cb)
beagle.http.bg:555

Read and decode a chunked-transfer-encoding body from the connection buffer.

read-chunked-loop(cb, out)
beagle.http.bg:562

Internal read loop for read-chunked-body.

read-request(cb)
beagle.http.bg:585

Read and parse a full HTTP request (request line, headers, and a Content-Length or chunked body if present) into a Request; null if no request line.

serialize-response(resp)
beagle.http.bg:634

Serialize a Response to a full HTTP/1.1 string, always setting Content-Length and Connection: close and emitting the user headers.

byte-length(s)
beagle.http.bg:671

Return the UTF-8 byte length of a string (codepoint count != bytes for non-ASCII), computed via a string-builder.

route(method, path, handler)
beagle.http.bg:683

Build a Route, uppercasing the method.

split-path-segments(p)
beagle.http.bg:690

Split a path into non-empty "/"-separated segments.

match-path(pattern, path)
beagle.http.bg:717

Match a route pattern against a path. Returns a params map (string->string) on match, or null. Supports :name params and a trailing *name wildcard.

match-segs(pat, pth, i, params)
beagle.http.bg:722

Internal recursive segment matcher for match-path.

query-params(req)
beagle.http.bg:758

Parse the request's query string into a decoded map (e.g. "a=1&b=2" -> {"a" "1", "b" "2"}). Empty/absent query yields {}.

query-param(req, name)
beagle.http.bg:768

Return the decoded value of query parameter name, or null if absent.

parse-cookie-header(h)
beagle.http.bg:789

Parse a Cookie header value ("a=1; b=2") into a map of cookie name -> value.

parse-cookie-pairs(h, start, acc)
beagle.http.bg:798

Internal: fold the "; "-separated pairs of a Cookie header into a map.

cookies(req)
beagle.http.bg:821

Parse the request's Cookie header into a map of cookie name -> value ({} if none).

cookie(req, name)
beagle.http.bg:826

Return the value of cookie name, or null if absent.

form-params(req)
beagle.http.bg:832

Parse the request body as application/x-www-form-urlencoded into a decoded map (string->string). Empty/absent body yields {}.

form-param(req, name)
beagle.http.bg:841

Return the decoded form field name, or null if absent.

with-params(req, params)
beagle.http.bg:847

Return a copy of req with params attached (used by the router).

router(routes, not-found-handler)
beagle.http.bg:868

Build a handler dispatching a vector of Route on method + path (with :name params and a trailing *name wildcard). 405 with an Allow header on a path-but-not-method match, otherwise the not-found-handler.

default-not-found(req)
beagle.http.bg:909

Default 404 handler returning "Not Found: <path>".

handle-connection(conn, handler)
beagle.http.bg:921

Serve one connection: read the request, run the handler (handler errors become a 500 so the loop survives), write the response, and close.

serve(host, port, handler)
beagle.http.bg:949

Run a cooperative single-thread HTTP server with the given handler (Request -> Response), serving one connection at a time on the calling thread (GC-safe). Blocks forever.

serve-routes(host, port, routes)
beagle.http.bg:967

Serve a vector of Route with the default 404 handler.

read-response(cb)
beagle.http.bg:980

Read and parse a full HTTP response (status line, headers, then body by Content-Length if present else until EOF) into a Response.

read-until-eof(cb)
beagle.http.bg:1021

Read and return everything remaining on the connection until EOF, draining any already-buffered bytes first.

build-request(method, host, path, headers, body)
beagle.http.bg:1063

Build the HTTP/1.1 request bytes for a method/host/path/headers/body.

request(method, url, opts)
beagle.http.bg:1105

Make an HTTP or HTTPS request to a full url and return the parsed Response. The URL scheme selects the transport (plain TCP for http, TLS for https) and the default port (80 / 443). opts is a map of options: :headers a map of request headers (default {}) :body a request body string (default "")

Examples: request("GET", "https://example.com/", {}) request("POST", "http://api.local/x", {:body "hi", :headers {"x-tok" "v"}})

8 undocumented
  • hex-val(c)
  • trim-spaces(s)
  • get(url)get(url, opts)arities 1, 2
  • post(url)post(url, opts)arities 1, 2
  • put(url)put(url, opts)arities 1, 2
  • patch(url)patch(url, opts)arities 1, 2
  • delete(url)delete(url, opts)arities 1, 2
  • head(url)head(url, opts)arities 1, 2

#beagle.ini

16/16 documented
ws?(c)
beagle.ini.bg:59

True if byte c is ASCII whitespace (space, tab, CR, or LF).

skip-ws-forward(s, start, end)
beagle.ini.bg:65

Index of the first non-whitespace byte in s[start:end], or end if none.

trim-ws-backward(s, start, end)
beagle.ini.bg:76

Index one past the last non-whitespace byte in s[start:end]; start if the range is all whitespace.

trim-range(s, start, end)
beagle.ini.bg:86

Trim ASCII whitespace from s[start:end] and return that substring.

unquote-value(v)
beagle.ini.bg:99

Strip one pair of surrounding double quotes from an already-trimmed value, returning the inner text; returns the value unchanged if it is not quoted.

find-separator(s, start, end)
beagle.ini.bg:110

Index of the first '=' or ':' in s[start:end], or -1 if neither occurs.

parse(text)
beagle.ini.bg:125

Parse INI/.conf text into a map of section-keyword -> property map (string keys and string values). Keys before any [section] live under :default. Accepts both '\n' and '\r\n' line endings.

parse-line(text, start, end, result, current)
beagle.ini.bg:160

Process one raw line (text[start:end], no terminator) against the running parse state. Handles blank lines, ';'/'#' comments, [section] headers, and key=value / key:value properties; returns [updated-result, updated-current-section].

value-needs-quoting?(s)
beagle.ini.bg:212

True if writing s needs surrounding quotes to survive a parse round-trip — i.e. it has leading/trailing whitespace or contains a double quote. Empty strings never need quoting.

write-value!(builder, s)
beagle.ini.bg:236

Append value s to the string builder, wrapping it in double quotes only when value-needs-quoting? is true. Returns null.

write-section-body!(builder, section)
beagle.ini.bg:249

Append one "key=value" line (LF-terminated) per entry of a section's property map to the builder, quoting values as needed. Returns null.

stringify(data)
beagle.ini.bg:270

Serialize a parsed-shape map back into INI text. The :default section, if present, is emitted first without a header; every other section gets a [name] header. Uses '\n' line endings and '=' separators, and re-quotes values only when needed so stringify(parse(x)) re-parses equal.

get-raw(ini, section, key)
beagle.ini.bg:317

Look up the raw string value for section/key in a parsed INI map, or null when the section or key is absent. section is the bare header string (e.g. "db").

get-bool(ini, section, key)
beagle.ini.bg:327

Read section/key from a parsed INI map as a Bool. Accepts (case-insensitively) true/false, yes/no, on/off, and 1/0. Returns null when absent; throws when the value is present but is not one of those.

get-int(ini, section, key)
beagle.ini.bg:344

Read section/key from a parsed INI map as an Int (optional leading '-'). Returns null when absent; throws when the value is present but is not a valid integer.

get-float(ini, section, key)
beagle.ini.bg:359

Read section/key from a parsed INI map as a Float (decimal/exponent forms accepted; an integer like "10" becomes 10.0). Returns null when absent; throws when the value is present but is not a valid number.

#beagle.ip

20/31 documented
parse-octet(s)
beagle.ip.bg:23

Parse a single decimal IPv4 octet string into an Int in [0,255].

Returns null for empty input, non-digit characters, more than 3 digits, values above 255, or a leading zero (octal-confusion guard); "0" is valid.

parse-ipv4(s)
beagle.ip.bg:65

Parse a dotted-quad IPv4 string into a 32-bit Int.

Returns null if the string is not a valid IPv4 address.

Examples: parse-ipv4("192.168.1.1") // => 3232235777 parse-ipv4("999.1.1.1") // => null

ipv4-to-string(n)
beagle.ip.bg:86

Convert a 32-bit Int into a dotted-quad IPv4 string.

Examples: ipv4-to-string(3232235777) // => "192.168.1.1"

prefix-to-mask(prefix)
beagle.ip.bg:100

Build a 32-bit IPv4 netmask Int from a prefix length in [0,32].

prefix 0 => 0; prefix 32 => 0xFFFFFFFF; values are clamped to that range.

cidr-network(cidr)
beagle.ip.bg:119

Parse a CIDR string ("a.b.c.d/prefix") into [network-int, mask-int].

Returns null if the CIDR is malformed.

Examples: cidr-network("192.168.1.0/24") // => [3232235776, 4294967040]

ipv4-in-cidr?(ip-str, cidr-str)
beagle.ip.bg:145

Check whether an IPv4 address string lies within a CIDR block.
